text: Hello. I'm sharing a case of a recent ileocolic resection. This is a patient with severe refractory Crohn's disease, maximal medical therapy now on steroids and symptoms that continue. So doing my standard ileocolic resection, this is three ports. It's umbilicus, it's right lower quadrant and it's suprapubic. I'm just taking a look at the, um, lay of the land here and now getting ready for my resection. I'm going to tent the ileocolic pedicle and do a medial to lateral dissection. Choosing to use the ligature for dissection here, given the inflamed tissues, I felt that this was giving better hemostasis during dissection. So we'll just get into that retroperitoneal plane and come out and around the iliocolic pedicle. Although this is not a cancer operation, there is a lot of evidence that doing more of a CME dissection can help prevent recurrence, and so I do make efforts to do more of an oncologic resection despite the fact that this is benign disease. In this particular patient with very favorable body habitus, this was not too much of a challenge to do.

The surgeon identifies enlarged lymph nodes in the ileocolic pedicle and uses a ligature to complete the dissection of the pedicle. The medial to lateral dissection continues, with minimal dissection across the hepatic flexure due to the low position of the flexure, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon marks the small bowel mesenteric transection, noting the thickened bowel and mesentery. Using ICG mode, the ureteral stent is visible, aiding in safe dissection without encroaching on the right ureter. The planned margin is assessed, and the surgeon prepares for lateral mobilization by finishing the colon mesentery dissection towards the ascending colon and hepatic flexure. The transverse colon is mobilized for intracorporeal anastomosis, aligning the bowel without exteriorization.

The surgeon navigates the thickened and inflamed area, likely from a previous perforation, using blunt dissection to separate granulation tissue from the retroperitoneum. The residual attachments to the small bowel mesentery are freed, and perfusion is checked using ICG mode. Clips are placed for marking before switching back to white light. The surgeon uses a stapler from the suprapubic port to divide the colon and small bowel, employing purple loads for the bowel division.

The surgeon lines up for the intracorporeal anastomosis, using a stay suture tethered to the abdominal wall and stapling through the suprapubic port site with a white load stapler. The common channel is sutured closed using a three OB lock suture in a lumbered fashion, demonstrating the baseball stitch technique.

The surgeon verifies the closure of the anastomosis by inspecting the area and ensuring complete perfusion. A dunking stitch is used to secure the closure, with the anastomosis appearing well-perfused. The surgeon meticulously sutures around and below the original corner, using a suspending stitch to elevate the tissue for better visualization and to ensure no gaps remain.
The surgeon completes the dissection and prepares to exteriorize the specimen from the pelvis through the suprapubic port site using a small Pfannenstiel incision with an Alexis wound protector.
